task_context.question: What is the most important building in the National Heritage for England located 4 mi southwest of Congleton in Cheshire?
task_context.agent_role: Produce one compact second-hop retrieval query targeting the missing bridge or supporting evidence.

visible_agent_inputs.question: What is the most important building in the National Heritage for England located 4 mi southwest of Congleton in Cheshire?
visible_agent_inputs.summary_1:
Buildings recorded on the National Heritage List near Congleton (Cheshire)
- All Saints Chapel, Somerford — "near the hamlet of Brereton Heath, between Congleton and Holmes Chapel"; designated Grade II* listed.
- Nether Alderley Mill — "16th-century watermill", "to the south of the village of Nether Alderley"; Grade II* listed; owned by the National Trust.
- Missing bridge fact: explicit relation "distance & direction = 4 mi southwest of Congleton" between Congleton and the single "most important" listed building is not stated in the passages. Possible bridge entities (unverified for that relation): All Saints Chapel, Somerford; Nether Alderley Mill.
- Next hops (priority order): 1) Fetch "All Saints Chapel, Somerford" — check lede/infobox and "Location"/"History" sections for coordinates or stated distance/direction from Congleton. 2) Fetch "Nether Alderley Mill" — check lede/infobox for coordinates/distance and the "Nether Alderley" village page lede for distance from Congleton. 3) If needed, query: "4 miles southwest of Congleton listed building" or search Wikipedia for "4 mi SW of Congleton" and check matching article ledes/infoboxes.

input_dict.current_prompt:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Priority rules for ambiguity and disambiguation:
- If summary_1 lists multiple candidate anchors or otherwise signals ambiguity, emit a single connector-style query that concatenates the prioritized anchor tokens (use the order or priority visible in summary_1) and then append exactly one short attribute token or a visible year token for disambiguation.
- If summary_1 contains a comma-separated list of individuals and the missing evidence concerns a member, prefer the earliest-listed individual token paired with the visible group token and append the single attribute token member.
- If the question contains a visible year token that disambiguates a title, include that year token before the attribute token in the query.
- If none of the above applies, emit a single-anchor query using the most relevant visible token and at most one short attribute token when the question implies an attribute.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.

retrieved_doc_snippets: All Saints Chapel, Somerford | All Saints Chapel, Somerford is in an isolated position near the hamlet of Brereton Heath, between Congleton and Holmes Chapel, Cheshire, England. It is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building. The chapel is in the Anglican benefice of Astbury and Smallwood in the deanery of Congleton, the archdeaconry of Macclesfield and the diocese of Chester.
All Saints Chapel | All Saints Chapel (or All Saints' Chapel) may refer to:

Cedar Grove Plantation Chapel | Cedar Grove Plantation Chapel, also known as Summer Chapel, All Saints' Episcopal Church, and Waccamaw, is a historic plantation chapel located near Pawleys Island, Georgetown County, South Carolina. It was built about 1850, and is a small, frame vernacular Gothic Revival style chapel. It features a pedimented portico supported by four, paneled, square columns. The chapel is associated with All Saints’ Episcopal Church.

Somerford Park, Cheshire | Somerford Park is situated off the A54 road midway between Congleton and Holmes Chapel in Cheshire. Somerfield Hall was a Georgian brick-built country house which used to stand in the park as the seat of the Shakerley family, but was demolished in 1926.

Royal Chapel of All Saints | The Royal Chapel of All Saints or Queen Victoria's Chapel is a Grade II listed church in the grounds of the Royal Lodge in Windsor Great Park, Berkshire, England. The chapel is situated in the grounds of Royal Lodge. The chapel is a Royal Peculiar, and serves as an informal parish church for the inhabitants and staff of the Windsor Great Park. Services at the chapel are often attended by members of the British Royal family, and the Queen regularly worships at the chu ...[truncated]

St. George's Chapel, Lewes | St. George's Chapel is a historic Episcopal chapel located near Angola, Sussex County, Delaware on the Indian River Hundred. It was built in 1794, and is a one-story brick structure measuring 42 feet by 32 feet. It has a brick tower at the southwest corner, built in 1955, and features a restored Palladian window. The original furnishings were removed in 1850 and the roof replaced in 1882 with a steep gable roof. The chapel was restored in 1966. It is joined with All  ...[truncated]

Chapel of All Saints, Tarnobrzeg | The Chapel of All Saints is located in Tarnobrzeg, Poland (Polish: "Kaplica Wszystkich Świętych w Tarnobrzegu" ) in Sobów Borough, on a municipal cemetery, on Litewska Street. The building was initiated by the provost of Serbinów parish of Our Lady of Perpetual Help Michał Józefczyk. Work started at the beginning of 2009, and ended at the end of October.
source_state.summary_2: All Saints Chapel, Somerford is a Grade II* listed building recorded in the National Heritage List for England. All Saints Chapel, Somerford is in an isolated position near the hamlet of Brereton Heath between Congleton and Holmes Chapel in Cheshire (the provided passages do not state that it is 4 miles southwest of Congleton).
